parsed.message:
The rust-coloured water pooling in your drip tray indicates that the geyser's internal steel cylinder has corroded through. This typically happens when the sacrificial anode, which is designed to protect the tank from mineral attack, has been completely consumed over years of service. Once the protective lining fails, the water directly attacks the steel shell, leading to pinhole leaks and structural failure of the vessel.

Because the tank is actively leaking and the water is pooling in the tray, this is a progressive issue that requires prompt attention. If left unresolved, the leak can worsen suddenly, potentially overwhelming the drip tray's drainage capacity and causing significant water damage to your ceilings and floorboards below.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will isolate the water and electrical supply to the geyser, drain the remaining water from the cylinder, and remove the failed unit. They will then install a new SABS-approved geyser, replace the safety valves, and ensure the drip tray and overflow piping are fully functional before issuing a Certificate of Compliance.

An under-sink or behind-wall view showing copper and white plastic pipes with a large damp, dark water stain on the wooden board below them.
An exterior view of an apartment building walkway, showing the outer wall where the geyser overflow and discharge pipes exit the building.

Install a new geyser unit, replace the temperature and pressure safety valves, and commission the system.

parsed.message:
The dark, rust-coloured water pooling in your drip tray indicates that the geyser's internal cylinder has corroded. This typically happens when the sacrificial anode, which is designed to protect the steel tank from rusting, has been completely consumed over time, allowing the water to attack the steel walls of the tank.

This is a progressive issue that will continue to degrade. If left unaddressed, the weakened steel can rupture completely, leading to a sudden and severe indoor flood that can collapse ceilings and damage your property's structure.

parsed.thought: Symmetry check: Comparing the two sides of the door in the wide shot reveals a major imbalance. The right side has a heavy vertical tension spring connected to the lifting bracket, while the left side has an empty bracket with no spring present. This missing spring forces the opener motor to lift the full weight of the heavy wooden door on its own. The motor detects this extreme resistance as an obstruction, which triggers its safety overload sensor to beep and reverse the door.

parsed.message:
The garage door is failing to open because the left-side tension spring is completely missing. On a tilt-up wooden garage door, these heavy springs on each side act as a counterbalance to offset the immense weight of the timber. Without the left spring, the door is severely unbalanced, forcing the ceiling-mounted motor to lift almost the entire weight of the wood on its own.

When the motor attempts to lift this unbalanced load, it quickly exceeds its safe operating current. The control board interprets this excessive resistance as an obstruction, causing the motor to stop, beep an error code, and reverse the door to prevent the drive gear from stripping or the motor winding from burning out.

A wide view of the entire wooden garage door from inside, showing the ceiling-mounted opener and revealing a vertical spring on the right side but none on the left.
A close-up of the ceiling rail and the red emergency release cord hanging down near the top edge of the wooden door.
A detailed view of the metal mounting plate bolted to the top centre of the wooden door frame, where the motor's drawbar connects.

Inspect the remaining right-side spring for metal fatigue or wear and replace it if necessary to ensure balanced tension.
Adjust and balance the tension on both sides so the door can be lifted easily by hand.
Test the opener motor's force limits and safety reverse settings to ensure smooth operation.

parsed.diy_verification: Pull the red emergency release cord to disconnect the motor, then try to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy and will not stay open on its own, the counterbalance system has failed.

parsed.thought: Symmetry check: Comparing the two sides of the garage door reveals that the left side bracket is completely missing its heavy tension spring, while the right side has its tension spring fully intact and under tension. This severe imbalance has caused the wooden door to hang crookedly and jam in a partially open position. The ceiling-mounted opener motor is now under excessive strain because it is lifting the full dead weight of the timber door without the necessary counterbalance. A garage door specialist will need to install a matching replacement tension spring on the left side and re-align the entire assembly.

parsed.message:
The garage door is hanging crookedly because the heavy tension spring on the left-hand side is completely missing. On tilt-up wooden garage doors, these large springs on either side act as a counterbalance to offset the immense weight of the timber. When one spring breaks or detaches, the door loses its balance, causing it to twist, jam in the tracks, and put severe strain on the opener motor.

This imbalance typically develops when the metal in the spring fatigues over years of daily tension cycles, eventually snapping under load. Operating the door in this unbalanced state can quickly burn out the opener motor or bend the central lifting arm, leading to much more expensive repairs.

Do not attempt to operate the door using the automated motor, as this can burn out the drive gear. Because these springs are under extreme tension when the door is moved, attempting to adjust or install them yourself poses a severe risk of impact injury.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will safely relieve any remaining tension on the right-hand spring before installing a matching replacement spring on the left side. They will then inspect the lifting arms, brackets, and tracks for any alignment issues or physical damage caused by the twist. Finally, they will balance the door manually to ensure smooth operation before reconnecting and testing the automated opener.
image_descriptions: A close-up of the left-hand side wall bracket showing the black pivot arm assembly attached to the wooden door, with the tension spring completely absent.
A wide view from inside the garage showing the wooden door hanging crookedly, with the right-hand tension spring visible while the left-hand spring is missing.
A view looking up at the central ceiling track and the red emergency release cord hanging down near the top edge of the wooden door.
A close-up of the silver mounting bracket bolted to the top center of the wooden door, showing the connection to the motor's pulling arm.
